title: State Department shutters global air pollution monitoring program - The Washington Post
description: The program had used sensors at more than 80 U.S. embassies and consulates to track air quality, mostly in places where such data was unavailable.
bodyText: The State Department on Tuesday halted efforts to monitor air quality levels around the world, ending a program that had provided data about a major global health risk. The program had used air quality sensors at more than 80 U.S. embassies and consulates — mostly in countries where such data was otherwise limited or unreliable. The move follows other actions by the Trump administration to curtail environmental monitoring and climate-related science. A State Department spokesperson confirmed the decision to suspend the program, which was first reported by the New York Times, in an email to The Washington Post late Tuesday. “Starting on March 4, the Department’s Air Quality Monitoring Program will no longer be able to transmit air pollution data from our embassies and consulates … due to budget constraints,” the spokesperson said. “The current budget climate requires us to make difficult cuts and unfortunately, we cannot continue to publish this data,” the spokesperson added. The data had been published on AirNow.gov, a website maintained by the Environmental Protection Agency, as well as on a mobile app called ZephAir. As of Tuesday evening, an AirNow webpage on data from U.S. embassies and consulates displayed an error message. “Sorry, But This Web Page Does Not Exist,” the message read. On Wednesday, ZephAir also displayed messages that read “No data” and “Monitor down.” It was unclear whether billionaire Elon Musk’s U.S. DOGE Service had played a role in the decision. DOGE — which stands for Department of Government Efficiency — has sought to dramatically slash spending across the federal government in recent weeks. The State Department spokesperson did not respond to follow-up requests for comment. A 2022 research paper found air pollution reductions of a “statistically significant level” in places where U.S. embassy monitors had been installed. Cities without U.S. monitoring did not show similar declines. The study said that U.S. efforts had succeeded in building awareness about air quality risks, with Google searches for terms like “air quality” increasing once the embassy gauges were in place. The program had originated with a single monitor, capable of detecting fine pollutants at hourly intervals, installed in 2008 on the roof of the U.S. Embassy in Beijing. Though the initial intent was to provide information for Americans and visitors, the information caught fire on the Chinese internet — and put pressure on Chinese officials over what they had dismissed as “excessive fog.” On one day in 2013, smog reached such levels that drivers needed to pull off roads. As fine particulate matter (PM2.5) levels soared, Beijing’s monitors topped out at 500 micrograms per cubic meter of air. But the U.S. Embassy monitor kept tracking the rise — all the way to an astonishing 755 micrograms per cubic meter of air. (Under President Joe Biden, the EPA finalized a U.S. smog standard of 9 micrograms per cubic meter.) By the next year, China had launched a “war against pollution.” Gary Locke, the U.S. ambassador to China during that period, said he’d never seen a U.S. initiative have such an “immediate, dramatic impact in a country.” The United States expanded the program in 2015 and eventually installed monitors in heavily polluted cities such as Mumbai; Karachi, Pakistan; and Dhaka, Bangladesh. Environmental and public health advocates say policymakers and donors have failed to prioritize the issue of air pollution. One report, from 2022, found that air pollution cuts more than two years off global average life expectancy, making it a more significant health problem than cigarettes.
